title: Dog Unresponsive When Called: Is it an Emergency? | Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is unresponsive when called, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes, urgency levels, and how to get help immediately.
content_html:
What Does it Mean if Your Dog is Unresponsive?

Unresponsiveness in dogs refers to a state where a pet fails to react to external stimuli that would normally trigger a response, such as hearing their name, loud noises, or physical touch. This condition can manifest as a deep stupor, a seizure, or a total loss of consciousness. It indicates that the brain or central nervous system is not functioning correctly, which can be caused by various internal or external factors.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. Sudden unresponsiveness is a high-priority emergency. If your dog does not wake up or react when called or touched, you must seek immediate veterinary care at the nearest emergency animal hospital. Potential causes include poisoning, heatstroke, heart failure, severe allergic reactions, or neurological trauma. Time is of the essence to ensure the best possible outcome for your pet.

Why Taking a Video or Photo Helps Triage

While your priority should be getting your dog to a veterinarian, capturing a brief video of the incident—if it is safe to do so—can be incredibly helpful for the medical team. A video allows the vet to see the dog's exact posture, breathing rhythm, and eye movement during the episode. Because these symptoms may change or subside by the time you arrive at the clinic, visual evidence helps the staff perform a more accurate and rapid triage to determine the necessary life-saving measures.

faqs.question: Should I try to wake my dog up with water?
faqs.answer: No. Never pour water on or into the mouth of an unresponsive dog, as this can lead to choking or aspiration pneumonia. Keep them calm and transport them immediately.

faqs.question: How can I check if my dog is breathing?
faqs.answer: Watch for the rise and fall of the chest or place a small piece of tissue near their nostrils to see if it moves. If they are not breathing, inform the vet clinic immediately while en route.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog on a flat, stable surface and ensure their neck is extended to keep the airway open. Call the emergency clinic while you are on the way so they can prepare for your arrival.
